findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Redesign ticket 7931's git-derivation readers to use the actual module-size mechanism (the single-integer `.github/module-size-limit.txt` + `git log --numstat` over `src/rebar/*.py`) rather than the removed `.github/module-size-ceilings.txt` / `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` files — both files are absent and a live test enforces their absence (`test_allowlist_mechanism_removed`); the 'allowlist count', 'total ceiling LOC', and 'new vs raised ceilings' metrics must be redefined against the surviving data model.
evidence: Ticket 7931 Approach: 'The ceilings ratchet already encodes the data: .github/module-size-ceilings.txt (<path> <max-lines>) and .github/module-size-allowlist.txt (bare paths) give the count + summed ceiling LOC by parsing; "new" vs "raised" is exactly a git log -p diff of the ceilings file'
Ticket 7931 ACs: 'total ceiling LOC equals the summed second column of a fixture ceilings file (assert total == 806+1093); allowlist count equals the fixture non-comment line count'

tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py:80-87 — CONTRADICTED: asserts .github/module-size-allowlist.txt and .github/module-size-ceilings.txt must NOT exist; test title: 'test_allowlist_mechanism_removed'
tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py:1-7: 'Epic 716f split every over-cap module and drained that allowlist to zero, then removed the escape hatch entirely'
.github/module-size-limit.txt: contains only the single integer '800' — not a per-module ratchet
Evidence class: Contradicted
findings.finding: The git-derivation readers (ticket 7931) and the epic Context/Decomposition section assert that `.github/module-size-ceilings.txt` and `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` exist and can be parsed to derive 'allowlisted-module count and total ceiling LOC, new vs raised ceilings' — but both files are definitively absent from the repo and a live test enforces their absence. `test_allowlist_mechanism_removed` (test_module_size_contract.py:82-87) asserts both MUST NOT exist because epic 716f removed the per-module ratchet and replaced it with a single absolute cap in `.github/module-size-limit.txt` (a single integer). The named source data for three of the plan's registered code-health metrics does not exist and cannot be reconstructed from what is present.
findings.location: ticket 7931 (git-derivation readers) — Approach section; epic Context section ('the two-file module-size ratchet'); epic Decomposition ('allowlisted-module count and total ceiling LOC, new vs raised ceilings')
scenarios: Ticket 7931 is implemented as written: the reader shells out to parse .github/module-size-ceilings.txt, finds it absent, and either crashes or silently produces no data — the three 'ceiling' metrics are permanently Unavailable with no upgrade path.

The 7931 tests create a fixture ceilings file in a temp dir and pass, while the live-repo assertions (test_allowlist_mechanism_removed) continue to assert the file must not exist — the integration test and the live-repo contract are in permanent contradiction.

The epic metric registry includes metrics (allowlist count, ceiling LOC, new/raised ceilings) whose source data is structurally absent, making those metric slots permanently Unavailable even as 'going-forward' entries.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the ceilings/allowlist-based metrics with metrics derivable from the existing absolute-cap model: (a) 'modules at or above N% of the 800-LOC cap' (computed from git log --numstat over src/rebar/), (b) 'churn rate' (deletions:insertions ratio from git log --numstat over the date range), (c) 'new modules added in range' (new *.py paths in git log --diff-filter=A). These are all computable from surviving data. Remove the fixture ACs that reference 'ceilings.txt' or 'allowlist.txt' from ticket 7931.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Change 5062's backfill label from `source=backfill_structural` to `source=backfill_classified` (the single canonical backfill vocabulary defined by the parent).
evidence: Parent SC5: 'every backfilled/classified value is labeled `source=backfill_classified` and `confidence=classified` (the single backfill vocabulary — there is no `backfill_structural`)'
5062 Approach: 'Backfilled classes carry `source=backfill_structural, confidence=classified`'
Parent AC-B: '`is_authoritative()` returns False for it so it is never aggregated into an authoritative structural series'
findings.finding: Child 5062 uses a forbidden `source=backfill_structural` label that the parent plan explicitly prohibits, stating 'there is no `backfill_structural`' and mandating a single backfill vocabulary of `source=backfill_classified, confidence=classified`.
findings.location: child 5062-30a8-9153-4c61, Approach section: 'carry `source=backfill_structural, confidence=classified`'
scenarios: The registry's is_authoritative() check tests for 'backfill_classified'; a 'backfill_structural' value would pass the authoritative filter and contaminate structural series
Parent AC-D integration test asserts `source=backfill_classified`; 5062's output would cause that test to fail
Any downstream consumer filtering on the canonical backfill label would miss 5062's classified records
findings.suggested_fix: In `scripts/backfill_bug_class.py` and the corresponding test, replace all occurrences of `source=backfill_structural` with `source=backfill_classified` to match the single backfill vocabulary mandated by the parent plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Label c595's one-time historical backfill snapshots `source=backfill_classified, confidence=classified` (or introduce a distinct `source=backfill_snapshot` that is_authoritative() returns False for), distinct from the going-forward snapshot label.
evidence: Parent SC5: 'every backfilled/classified value is labeled `source=backfill_classified` and `confidence=classified`'
c595 Approach: 'snapshots are labeled `source=snapshot, confidence=high`'
Parent SC5: '`is_authoritative()` returns False for it so it is never aggregated into an authoritative structural series'
c085 registry: `is_authoritative` returns True for `source=snapshot` (snapshot is a structural source)
findings.finding: Child c595 labels one-time historical backfill snapshots as `source=snapshot, confidence=high` instead of `source=backfill_classified, confidence=classified`, making them appear authoritative when the parent requires all backfilled values to be non-authoritative.
findings.location: child c595-9348-d4f2-4529, Approach section: 'snapshots are labeled `source=snapshot, confidence=high` (deterministic tool output)'
scenarios: Backfilled complexity/clone data from c595 would be aggregated into authoritative series, inflating historical code-health confidence
Parent AC-D test asserting backfill_classified label would not be satisfiable by c595 output alone
Going-forward snapshot writes (genuinely current) are indistinguishable from historical backfill writes if both use source=snapshot
findings.suggested_fix: In `scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py`, write records with `source=backfill_classified, confidence=classified` rather than `source=snapshot, confidence=high`. The going-forward writer in 3275/1f77 keeps `source=snapshot`; only the one-time historical backfill uses the backfill label.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix 1f77's `test.yml` step to append coverage to `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` (the git-tracked path), not `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` (gitignored).
evidence: 1f77 AC#3: 'the `test.yml` change appends coverage to `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`'

Parent plan Snapshot store path note: 'The going-forward snapshot series (3275) is committed to `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` — NOT under `reports/`, which is gitignored (`.gitignore:74`) and would silently drop every snapshot'
3275 Approach: 'It lives under the tracked `.rebar/` store, NOT under `reports/`: `/reports/` is gitignored'
findings.finding: Child 1f77 specifies that the `test.yml` step appends coverage to `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`, which is a gitignored path. The parent plan and child 3275 explicitly establish that snapshots must live under `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` (a git-tracked path); `reports/` is gitignored at `.gitignore:74` and would silently discard every snapshot.
findings.location: child 1f77-f8c0-bfae-4a79, Acceptance Criteria #3: 'the `test.yml` change appends coverage to `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`'
scenarios: Every CI run appends to a gitignored file; the file is never committed; the coverage trend series never accumulates; 3c07 and 9a5a find no snapshot history for coverage metrics
A developer inspecting `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` sees only locally-run snapshots, not CI ones
findings.suggested_fix: In `.github/workflows/test.yml`, change the snapshot-append step's target path from `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` to `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`. Update 1f77's AC#3 to assert the added step targets the `.rebar/` path.

findings.finding: Child 5062 persists backfill labels as `source=backfill_structural, confidence=classified`, but the parent explicitly states 'the single backfill vocabulary — there is no `backfill_structural`', and c085 enumerates the allowed source values as {structural, git, sidecar, snapshot, backfill_classified}. `backfill_structural` is absent from that set, so 5062's output would be unrecognized by the registry and `is_authoritative()` logic.
findings.location: child 5062-30a8-9153-4c61 — Approach section ('source=backfill_structural, confidence=classified') and AC2
findings.suggested_fix: In scripts/backfill_bug_class.py and test_backfill_bug_class.py replace every occurrence of source='backfill_structural' with source='backfill_classified'. Update 5062 AC2 to assert source='backfill_classified' specifically, not just 'source is present'.

findings.finding: Child c595 writes backfill snapshots labeled `source=snapshot, confidence=high`, which `is_authoritative()` in c085 would classify as authoritative — yet these are one-time historical backfill values checked out from old commits. This contradicts the parent success criterion SC5 ('every backfilled/classified value is labeled source=backfill_classified and confidence=classified') and the parent AC3 segregation guarantee.
findings.location: child c595-9348-d4f2-4529 — Approach section ('source=snapshot, confidence=high') and AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Label c595 backfill snapshots source='backfill_classified', confidence='classified' (consistent with parent SC5). If deterministic tool-output backfill warrants a higher confidence, define a distinct source label (e.g. backfill_deterministic) in c085's vocabulary and update is_authoritative() to return False for it, then update c595's AC1 to assert that label.

findings.finding: Child 1f77 AC3 specifies that the `test.yml` change 'appends coverage to `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`', which is the gitignored path. The parent explicitly states the snapshot store is `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` (a git-tracked path) and that `/reports/` is gitignored (`.gitignore:74`) so snapshots there are silently dropped. Child 3275 correctly targets `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`.
findings.location: child 1f77-f8c0-bfae-4a79 — AC3 ('appends coverage to reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson')
findings.suggested_fix: In scripts/harvest_gha.py and the test.yml step, replace the snapshot output path reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson with .rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson. Update 1f77 AC3 to assert the added step targets .rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson.

findings.finding: Child 5062 labels backfilled close_class records `source=backfill_structural` but the parent plan explicitly states there is no `backfill_structural` and defines a single backfill vocabulary of `source=backfill_classified`. This contradicts the parent's SC5 and AC3/AC5, and means 5062's output may be treated as authoritative by c085's `is_authoritative()` helper (which only excludes `backfill_classified`), polluting the authoritative series.
findings.location: child 5062-30a8-9153-4c61, Approach section: 'Backfilled classes carry `source=backfill_structural, confidence=classified`'
findings.suggested_fix: In `scripts/backfill_bug_class.py` and `tests/unit/scripts/test_backfill_bug_class.py`, replace every occurrence of `source=backfill_structural` with `source=backfill_classified` to match the parent-mandated single vocabulary.

findings.finding: Child c595 labels backfill complexity/clone snapshots `source=snapshot, confidence=high`, but these are historical backfilled values and the parent requires ALL backfilled values carry `source=backfill_classified, confidence=classified`. Using `source=snapshot` causes them to be treated as authoritative by c085's `is_authoritative()`, violating the parent's segregation guarantee for backfill data.
findings.location: child c595-9348-d4f2-4529, Approach section: 'snapshots are labeled `source=snapshot, confidence=high`'
findings.suggested_fix: In `scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py`, write snapshots with `source=backfill_classified, confidence=classified` (matching the parent's single backfill vocabulary). Update c595's AC to assert those labels, and update c085's is_authoritative() to exclude backfill_classified consistently.

evidence: docs/ticket-model.md: 0b96-cbcb-0ea2-479d & ed13-e224-1ece-44ff (no ordering edge)
src/rebar/_cli/help/overview.txt: 0b96-cbcb-0ea2-479d & 9a5a-d9ea-47c1-4e6a (no ordering edge)
src/rebar/_commands/transition.py: 555e-3efa-4ad6-4169 & ed13-e224-1ece-44ff (no ordering edge)
src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py: 555e-3efa-4ad6-4169 & ed13-e224-1ece-44ff (no ordering edge)
tests/golden/cli_help/__overview_help.out: 0b96-cbcb-0ea2-479d & 9a5a-d9ea-47c1-4e6a (no ordering edge)
findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: The parent AC4 requires verifying the bug-close `--help` diff against a stored pre-epic baseline ('verified by diffing `rebar transition --help` against the pre-epic baseline'). No child AC owns this explicit snapshot/diff-against-baseline check; ed13's ACs test the behavioral outcome (close exits nonzero without --class) but do not capture or compare a stored pre-epic help snapshot.
findings.location: child ed13-e224-1ece-44ff — AC set; parent AC4
findings.suggested_fix: In tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_bug_close_class.py add a test that runs `rebar transition --help`, parses the required-argument list for the bug-close path, and asserts the count equals 1 (--class replacing --reason). Optionally check a stored golden baseline file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen the `--reason` removal verification in AC 4 to confirm `--reason` is absent (not merely demoted) — e.g., assert that `rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --class regression --reason foo` exits non-zero or that `--reason` does not appear in `rebar transition --help` output at all.
evidence: AC: 'a rebar transition --help diff shows no net increase in required bug-close arguments'
AC: 'verified by rebar show <closed-bug> --output json | jq -e .close_class'
The 'no net increase' check passes even if --reason is demoted to optional rather than removed entirely.
E1 criterion: 'for migrations, criteria verify BOTH removal and replacement'
findings.finding: The AC for the `--reason` → `--class` migration verifies replacement (via `jq -e '.close_class'`) and argument-count parity (via `--help` diff showing 'no net increase'), but does not verify that `--reason` is actually REMOVED from the required-argument set — it could remain as an optional argument, satisfying the count check while leaving the old interface partially intact.
findings.location: Success Criteria ('Bug-close friction stays flat'), AC bullet 4
scenarios: Implementer makes `--reason` optional instead of removing it; `--help` diff shows no net increase in *required* args; `close_class` field is present; both AC checks pass; but `--reason` is still accepted and silently ignored, leaving the migration incomplete.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC 4: 'AND `rebar transition --help` output contains no `--reason` argument for the bug-close path' or 'AND passing `--reason` to a bug close exits non-zero with an unrecognized-argument error.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or a named section in AGENTS.md / a design doc) covering: (1) the rationale and invariants for the direct `_write_link_event` bypass (closed source, non-cycle-inducing), (2) the `is_authoritative()` / confidence-vocabulary contract and its exhaustive enum, and (3) the metric-registry `unavailable` state protocol — so these decisions are discoverable by future contributors without reading this epic's plan prose.
evidence: "555e writes the edge via `graph._links._write_link_event(bug, culprit, "caused_by", ...)` **directly**, bypassing the closed-source and cycle guards"
"is_authoritative() returns False for it so it is never aggregated into an authoritative structural series"
"The command is built once against a complete metric registry: metrics whose signals have not yet accrued report a structured `unavailable` state"
No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc artifact anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan introduces at least three novel architectural decisions — (1) the direct `graph._links._write_link_event` bypass of the closed-source and cycle guards, (2) the `is_authoritative()` / `backfill_classified` confidence-vocabulary contract between the backfill subsystem and the metrics aggregation layer, and (3) the metric-registry `unavailable` state protocol — but names no ADR, AGENTS.md section, or design-doc update to capture any of them. Future maintainers seeing the `_write_link_event` direct call could "fix" it by routing through the public guard API, silently dropping all auto-derived edges; the `is_authoritative()` contract is only defined in this plan's prose.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Auto-link vs the closed-source guard (555e ordering)' paragraph; Success Criteria — 'is_authoritative() returns False'; Decomposition — 'metric-registry + unavailable/label contract'
scenarios: A future contributor sees the direct `_write_link_event` call in `transition_close.py`, treats it as a bug (bypassing a guard looks wrong without context), routes it through `add_dependency`, and silently kills all auto-derived `caused_by` edges — the feature becomes a dead letter with no test failure because the skip-on-guard-rejection path is indistinguishable from the skip-on-no-blame path.

A future contributor adding a new confidence tier finds `is_authoritative()` only implicitly defined (it returns False for `backfill_classified`) and cannot tell whether the contract is exhaustive or what other callers depend on it, leading to an incorrectly authoritative backfill series.
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/NNN-metrics-instrumentation.md` (or a `## Architecture` section in AGENTS.md) with three numbered decisions: ADR-1 explains the `_write_link_event` direct-write invariant and when it is safe to bypass guards; ADR-2 defines the `confidence` vocabulary exhaustively (`structural`, `classified`) and specifies `is_authoritative()` as returning True iff `confidence == 'structural'`; ADR-3 documents the metric-registry `unavailable` protocol contract. Reference this ADR from the relevant source files via a code comment.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the post-rollback schema state explicitly: specify whether `_fold_close_class` is retained (harmless, recommended) or removed on rollback, and confirm that STATUS events already written with `close_class` will (or will not) surface in `rebar show` after rollback.
evidence: Plan text: 'back-out is simply ceasing to require/write the field'
ed13 description: 'Additive/optional schema field (readers tolerate absence); back-out = stop requiring/writing it.'

The plan asserts only the forward-compat direction ('old STATUS records without close_class still reduce cleanly'), not the post-rollback state when STATUS records WITH close_class are present under a rolled-back reader.
ABSENCE: no plan text states whether `_fold_close_class` is retained or removed on rollback, or what `rebar show <ticket>` returns for a ticket closed with `close_class` after rollback.
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the schema/reduced state AFTER rollback of `ed13` when STATUS events carrying `close_class` have already been committed to the event log. The stated rollback ('stop requiring/writing it') is ambiguous: if the `_fold_close_class` processor is also removed during rollback, pre-rollback events' `close_class` data is orphaned (silently absent from reduced state); if the fold is retained but the CLI requirement dropped, the fold is harmless but the plan doesn't say which rollback state is reached.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Back-out / expand-contract for the bug-close CLI change (ed13)'; ed13 child ticket Approach: 'back-out = stop requiring/writing it'
scenarios: ed13 is landed, several bugs are closed with --class regression, then the feature is rolled back by reverting ed13. If the revert removes `_fold_close_class`, those tickets' close_class is silently dropped from reduced state — the events persist but the data disappears from `rebar show`. If the revert retains the fold, state is correct but the plan doesn't say so.
A downstream consumer that keyed on `close_class` presence breaks after rollback without a DATA LOSS RISK note in the plan.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach rollback description: 'On rollback, the `_fold_close_class` processor is RETAINED (it is a no-op for events without the field); STATUS events already written with `close_class` continue to surface correctly in `rebar show`. The only rollback action is removing the --class CLI requirement and the write of `close_class` into `status_data`. No event-log data is lost or orphaned.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a write-serialization strategy for `write_snapshot` (e.g., use the existing rebar write lock, a per-file advisory lock, or serialize via git commit) so concurrent CI runs cannot interleave NDJSON lines.
evidence: 3275 Approach: '`write_snapshot(dict)` appends a timestamped record to a committed NDJSON file'
No lock, no atomic-append, and no git-commit serialization is described for the append operation in the snapshot writer.

The existing event-write infrastructure uses `_acquire_write_lock` (txn.py) for all STATUS events, but snapshot.py is described as a separate new module with no stated integration with the write-lock mechanism.
3275 AC: 'a corrupt/truncated NDJSON line is skipped' — this AC acknowledges the possibility of corruption but treats it as a read-side concern rather than a write-side prevention.
findings.finding: The `write_snapshot` function appends to the shared `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` file. The plan describes no write-serialization mechanism (lock, git-commit serialization, or atomic-append guarantee) to handle concurrent CI runs both attempting to append to the file at the same time, risking interleaved or truncated lines.
findings.location: Child ticket 3275 — 'Going-forward metrics snapshot writer/reader (NDJSON)'; `src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py` — `write_snapshot(dict)`
scenarios: Two parallel CI jobs (e.g., coverage and complexity steps) both call `write_snapshot` within the same second; without a lock, one write can overwrite or truncate the other's line, producing a corrupt record.
A CI job is killed mid-append, leaving a partial (truncated) JSON line; the reader skips it, silently losing that snapshot point.
findings.suggested_fix: Integrate `write_snapshot` with the existing `.rebar/` write-lock infrastructure (the same `_acquire_write_lock` used by `txn.py`), or document that the snapshot writer uses Python's `fcntl.flock` for an advisory exclusive lock before appending.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For each optional backfill adapter ('GitHub-Actions coverage harvest', 'local Claude-Code-transcript mining'), add a one-line note in the plan stating: the specific API endpoint or filesystem path targeted, the auth precondition (e.g. `GITHUB_TOKEN` with `actions:read`), and the expected data schema — or explicitly defer each to a spike before implementation begins.
evidence: Plan quote: 'local Claude-Code-transcript mining (optional adapter); GitHub-Actions coverage + CI-health harvest (optional adapter)'
No search hit for 'GitHub Actions API' or 'Claude-Code transcript' in the repo — these are novel, not previously-used integrations.
Plan also states: 'harness/CI-specific backfills are isolated optional adapters the core command never depends on' — partially mitigating blast radius.
Evidence class: Unverified (adapters are optional and non-blocking for core delivery, but the external surfaces themselves are unverified)
findings.finding: Two backfill adapters named in the Decomposition section — 'local Claude-Code-transcript mining' and 'GitHub-Actions coverage + CI-health harvest' — reference external integrations with no named API endpoint, no auth/credential precondition, and no capability probe stated in the plan. The GitHub Actions artifact-download surface in particular requires a specific REST endpoint, a GITHUB_TOKEN with `actions:read` scope, and has per-repo retention-window constraints; none of these are specified.
findings.location: Epic Decomposition section — 'Backfill (one-time, labeled)' track: 'local Claude-Code-transcript mining (optional adapter); GitHub-Actions coverage + CI-health harvest (optional adapter)'
scenarios: The GitHub Actions coverage adapter is implemented and targets a deprecated or renamed endpoint (e.g. artifacts API path changed), causing silent data loss that is only caught when the optional adapter is exercised.
The Claude-Code transcript mining adapter assumes a specific filesystem path or JSON schema for transcripts that differs from the actual on-disk format, producing no data silently.

findings.finding: The plan's rollback description for `caused_by` links ('back-out is removing the additive links') and for `close_class` ('back-out = stop requiring/writing the field') does not assert the schema state AFTER rollback — specifically, already-written LINK events with `caused_by` and STATUS events carrying `close_class` remain permanently in the event store, and no tooling or procedure is named to remove them.
findings.location: Approach § 'back-out / expand-contract for the bug-close CLI change' and ticket 0b96 Approach § 'back-out is simply ceasing to emit the value'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to 0b96 and ed13 confirming that reduce-time validation is permissive for unknown enum values (cite the specific code path), and add a `scripts/purge_caused_by_links.py --dry-run` script reference as the compensating-forward path so rollback instructions are complete.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a consumer enumeration step to ed13: grep for `--reason` in all test files, scripts, and CI workflows; update each found caller to use `--class` before landing ed13.
evidence: ed13 Approach: '`--class` is REQUIRED on bug close (mirrors the old required `--reason`): omitting it exits nonzero'
ed13 AC: verifies `rebar transition --help` diff but does not enumerate existing callers of `--reason`
555e AC#1 correctly passes `--class regression` — consistent
Parent note: 'Any 555e/2f8e test that closes a bug MUST pass `--class`' — addresses 555e/2f8e but not all consumers
2f8e backfill script closes no tickets (read-only), so it is safe; 538c does not close tickets either
findings.finding: Child ed13 replaces `--reason` with `--class` on bug close, but neither ed13 nor any sibling enumerates and migrates existing callers of `rebar transition <bug> ... closed --reason <x>` (integration tests, scripts, CI steps). Callers not updated will break with a nonzero exit after ed13 lands.
findings.location: child ed13-e224-1ece-44ff — Approach and AC sections; sibling children 2f8e, 538c, 555e
scenarios: An existing integration test or CI script calls `rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --reason 'fixed'`; after ed13 it exits nonzero, breaking CI
A human operator with a documented workflow using `--reason` gets an unexpected failure
findings.suggested_fix: In ed13's test plan, add a search for `transition.*closed.*--reason` or `--reason.*closed` across the codebase and enumerate them in the child's files list so they are explicitly migrated.

findings.finding: The plan asserts git-blame-based `caused_by` auto-derivation (`git blame <fix>~1`) as a capability, but the codebase has zero git-blame invocations anywhere (authorship.py, attest/, gitutil.py all use git-log, never git-blame). The source-repo working directory for the blame call is unspecified — rebar is harness-agnostic and the tickets branch is a separate orphan tree from the source repo. No prior-art search or proof-of-concept validates that the subprocess shape, porcelain output parsing, and working-directory resolution work for the agent-harness deployments rebar targets.
findings.location: Approach section: 'Blame-derivation is best-effort and non-authoritative' + 'Auto-link vs the closed-source guard (555e ordering)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an expand-contract step for the `--reason` removal: either (a) accept both `--reason` (deprecated, silently ignored or mapped) and `--class` during a transition window with a deprecation warning, or (b) document that `rebar transition --help` diff will show `--reason` replaced and provide an explicit caller-migration note in the ed13 AC — the current back-out description covers only rolling back `--class`, not migrating callers off `--reason`.
evidence: 'the change swaps one required arg for another, adds none' — acknowledges the swap but not the migration path for `--reason` callers
'back-out is simply ceasing to require/write the field' — describes rolling back `--class`, not migrating away from `--reason`
'its error message must enumerate the allowed values so any existing caller that omitted it fails loudly' — addresses omitted-arg callers, not callers passing the old `--reason` arg
No mention of a transition window where both `--reason` and `--class` are accepted, nor a `rebar transition --help` deprecation notice for `--reason`
findings.finding: The `--reason` → `--class` swap removes an existing required CLI argument that current callers (scripts, CI pipelines, other tooling) may be passing. The plan acknowledges the swap and describes rolling back `--class` ('back-out is simply ceasing to require/write the field'), but it does NOT provide an expand-contract sequence or deprecation path for callers currently invoking `rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --reason <text>` — those callers will receive an unrecognized-argument error with no migration guidance the moment ed13 lands.
findings.location: Approach — 'Back-out / expand-contract for the bug-close CLI change (ed13)'; AC 'Bug-close friction stays flat'
scenarios: A CI script calling `rebar transition BUG-42 in_progress closed --reason 'fixed in PR #99'` breaks with 'unrecognized arguments: --reason' immediately after ed13 lands, with no actionable migration message.
An automated harness that wraps `rebar transition` inherits the break with no warning period.
findings.suggested_fix: In the ed13 AC, add: 'If `--reason` is supplied without `--class`, emit a deprecation warning mapping the free-text reason to `other` and accept the close for one release cycle; after that window, error with `--reason is removed; use --class <enum>`. Alternatively, document that `--reason` is being hard-removed and add a grep/audit step in the cross-ticket coordination note to identify any existing callers before landing.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pre-write idempotency check in the `caused_by` auto-link path (555e): before calling `_write_link_event` directly, call `_is_active_link(bug, culprit, 'caused_by', tracker_dir)` and skip the write if the edge already exists — matching the guard `add_dependency` applies at line 256 of `_links.py`.
evidence: `_links.py:256`: `if _is_active_link(source_id, target_id, relation, tracker_dir):` ... `return` — this idempotency guard is inside `add_dependency`, not inside `_write_link_event`.
`_links.py:108-139`: `_write_link_event` has no idempotency check — it appends unconditionally via `_seam.append_event`.
Plan text: 'bypassing the closed-source and cycle guards — which add nothing for a non-blocking, non-cycle-inducing relation on a closed source' — idempotency is not mentioned as also-bypassed.
findings.finding: The `_write_link_event` bypass in the `caused_by` auto-derivation path (555e) skips not only the closed-source and cycle guards — which the plan explicitly justifies — but also the `_is_active_link` idempotency guard that lives in `add_dependency` (line 256 of `_links.py`). If the close path is retried after a partial failure (e.g., the STATUS event committed but the auto-link step failed mid-execution and the caller retries the close), `_write_link_event` will write a duplicate `caused_by` edge. The plan discusses the bypass's rationale exclusively in terms of defeating the closed-source and cycle guards and does not address this idempotency gap.
findings.location: Approach section ('Auto-link vs the closed-source guard'); ticket 555e description ('writes the edge via graph._links._write_link_event(bug, culprit, "caused_by", ...) **directly**')
scenarios: The STATUS event commits (bug is now closed) but the auto-link subprocess or seam write fails; the caller (or a harness retry) re-invokes the close command; the second run re-executes the auto-link path and writes a second LINK event for the same caused_by edge — producing duplicate edges in the store.
A test fixture that closes the same bug twice (e.g., testing that a duplicate close is a no-op via the status no-op branch, then a different path) could leave a spurious extra caused_by edge.
findings.suggested_fix: In `transition_close.py` (the auto-link site, after `txn.transition_core` commits), wrap the `_write_link_event` call: `if not graph._links._is_active_link(bug_id, culprit_id, 'caused_by', tracker_dir): graph._links._write_link_event(bug_id, culprit_id, 'caused_by', tracker_dir)`. Same guard for the `--caused-by` override path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-paragraph prior-art ruling in the Approach section explaining why a thin adapter to an existing dev-analytics tool (DevLake, LinearB, Swarmia) was rejected in favor of the bespoke metric registry — the natural reason (rebar's event store is a custom git-backed NDJSON format not natively ingestible by standard tools) should be stated explicitly, not left implicit.
evidence: Plan uses DevLake/Swarmia to JUSTIFY the design, not to document why they were ruled out as targets

Rebar's event store is a custom git-backed NDJSON format not natively ingestible by standard tools — this is the natural justification for a bespoke layer, but it is never stated explicitly in the plan
The plan introduces MetricSpec, REGISTRY, is_authoritative(), Unavailable — all functionality that existing analytics systems provide out of the box
ABSENCE: no sentence explaining why 'write an adapter to feed DevLake/LinearB' was rejected
findings.finding: The plan cites DevLake and Swarmia to validate the layered-hybrid approach but never evaluates why a thin adapter to an existing dev-analytics tool was ruled out rather than building a bespoke metric registry, reader, and snapshot store. For a novel aggregation layer inside a ticket tool, prior-art consideration requires not just naming but eliminating the established approach.
findings.location: Approach section: 'the layered-hybrid design that dev-analytics (DevLake, Swarmia) and agent-observability practice converge on'
scenarios: A reviewer or future maintainer cannot determine whether the custom aggregation layer was chosen deliberately over established tools or whether those tools were simply not evaluated
If an external analytics tool is adopted later, the bespoke registry becomes dead weight with no documented rationale for its existence
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'Off-the-shelf dev-analytics tools (DevLake, LinearB, Swarmia) ingest from standardized API sources (GitHub, Jira, GitLab); rebar's event store is a custom git-backed HLC-timestamped NDJSON format with no standard connector, so a thin adapter would require building the same derivation logic we build here anyway — there is no leverage from adopting an external aggregation tier for the structural readers. The optional backfill adapters (GitHub Actions, Claude Code transcript) are isolated from the core command precisely so a future external-tool integration could replace them.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the one-time backfill runner is batched/chunked (e.g., processes N bugs per run, commits intermediate state) and is idempotent/resumable (re-running after a partial failure does not double-apply already-processed records).
evidence: Plan text: 'Backfill (one-time, labeled): caused_by over existing bugs; historical bug --class from structural signals; complexity/clone snapshots at sampled commits; local Claude-Code-transcript mining (optional adapter); GitHub-Actions coverage + CI-health harvest (optional adapter).'
No batch size, throttle, or chunking mechanism is described anywhere in the epic plan or in the blame-derivation child ticket (555e).

blame.py description: 'Blames the pre-fix state — the parent of the fixing commit (<fix>~1) — over the bug's recorded file_impact' — this git-blame invocation runs per closed bug in the backfill pass.
findings.finding: The one-time backfill track (caused_by over all existing closed bugs via git blame, close_class inference over all historical events, complexity/clone snapshots at sampled commits) specifies no batching or throttling. A large repo with many closed bugs makes git blame per-bug-per-file an unbounded single-shot job.
findings.location: Decomposition section — 'Backfill (one-time, labeled)' track; also epic Success Criteria: 'Historical data is backfilled wherever a durable source exists'
scenarios: A repo with 200+ closed bugs triggers 200+ git blame subprocess calls in one unthrottled loop, taking minutes and blocking CI.
If the backfill is interrupted mid-run (OOM, timeout, CI cancellation) there is no stated progress checkpoint to resume from, so the entire set must be reprocessed from scratch.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the backfill child ticket's Approach: 'The backfill script processes bugs in batches of N (configurable, default 50); after each batch it commits intermediate LINK events so a re-run skips already-linked pairs via the existing `_is_active_link` idempotency check. The close_class inference similarly skips tickets whose reduced state already carries a `close_class` field.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `close_class` enum $def to `src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json` (mirroring how `relation` and `ticket_status` are defined), reference it from `ticket_state.schema.json`, and derive a single Python constant (e.g., `VALID_CLOSE_CLASSES: frozenset`) from which both txn.py and transition_close.py guard sites draw — so the vocabulary has exactly one authoritative source.
evidence: ed13 Approach: 'Closed MECE vocabulary with an escape + hygiene values: regression, plan_defect, env_integration, flaky, preexisting, not_a_bug, duplicate, escalated, undetermined'
ed13 Files section: adds optional close_class to ticket_state.schema.json but does NOT add a close_class enum $def to common.schema.json
epic plan: 'its error message must enumerate the allowed values so any existing caller that omitted it fails loudly with an actionable message'
src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json: existing enums (ticket_status, relation) are defined as $defs here with explicit enum arrays — no close_class $def is planned for this canonical location
Both txn.py and transition_close.py guard sites must validate against this vocabulary; no shared constant is specified
findings.finding: The `close_class` enum vocabulary (9 values: regression, plan_defect, env_integration, flaky, preexisting, not_a_bug, duplicate, escalated, undetermined) is defined only in ed13 prose and never placed in a machine-readable schema constant (e.g., a `close_class` $def in `common.schema.json` or a Python-level `VALID_CLOSE_CLASSES` frozenset). Both validation guard sites (txn.py and transition_close.py) and the error message that 'must enumerate the allowed values' must agree on the same set, but without a single authoritative source they can drift independently.
findings.location: ed13 ticket / Approach section ('Closed MECE vocabulary...'); epic Success Criteria ('error message must enumerate the allowed values')
scenarios: An implementer hardcodes 8 values in txn.py and 9 in transition_close.py (drift); the error message lists a different subset.
A future value is added to one guard site but not the other, or the schema optional field has no enum constraint for JSON Schema validation.
findings.suggested_fix: In common.schema.json add: `"close_class": {"type": "string", "enum": ["regression", "plan_defect", "env_integration", "flaky", "preexisting", "not_a_bug", "duplicate", "escalated", "undetermined"]}` under `$defs`; reference it from ticket_state.schema.json; add `VALID_CLOSE_CLASSES = frozenset({...})` to a shared constants module and import it at both guard sites.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `SOURCE_VOCABULARY: frozenset` and `CONFIDENCE_VOCABULARY: frozenset` as explicit Python constants in `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py` (or a shared constants module), and have `is_authoritative(source)` check against `SOURCE_VOCABULARY` rather than a hardcoded inline set, so the vocabulary has a single authoritative definition for all consumers.
evidence: c085 Approach: 'source in {structural, git, sidecar, snapshot, backfill_classified} and confidence in {high, classified} ride on every value'

c085 AC: 'is_authoritative("backfill_classified") is False and an authoritative aggregation excludes classified values — unit test asserts a mixed input yields a series containing only source in {structural,git,sidecar,snapshot}'
No schema file or code constant is planned for these vocabularies in c085 Files section (only registry.py, __init__.py)
Epic Success Criteria: 'every backfilled/classified value is labeled source=backfill_classified and confidence=classified' — only enforceable if the vocabulary is machine-readable
findings.finding: The `source` and `confidence` label vocabularies for the metric registry (source ∈ {structural, git, sidecar, snapshot, backfill_classified}; confidence ∈ {high, classified}) are defined only in the c085 ticket description prose and never placed in a schema $def, Python Literal/frozenset, or any machine-readable constant. The `is_authoritative(source)` helper and any aggregation logic must agree on this set, but with no canonical definition the set can drift between the registry, the helper, and downstream consumers.
findings.location: c085 ticket / Approach section; epic Success Criteria ('source and confidence' labels)
scenarios: A reader registers a new source value (e.g., 'ci_harvest') that is not in the authoritative set; is_authoritative() silently treats it as authoritative.
An implementer adds 'confidence=medium' as a third confidence tier in a new reader; the aggregation guard misses it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test in 555e's integration suite that explicitly verifies the `_write_link_event` path for `caused_by` does not block dependency resolution and does not raise on a two-node cycle, complementing 0b96's add_dependency test.
evidence: 0b96 Tests: 'a `caused_by` two-cycle is allowed (asserts the `_graph.py` semantics)' — tests add_dependency path
555e Approach: 'The auto-link therefore writes via `graph._links._write_link_event(...) **directly**, bypassing the closed-source and cycle guards'
If _write_link_event bypasses all guards, then 0b96's non-blocking tuple change in _graph.py is irrelevant for the 555e path — and 0b96's test proves correctness of a code path that 555e never uses
findings.finding: Child 0b96's unit test for the non-blocking/non-cycle semantics exercises the `add_dependency` code path, but child 555e's auto-link uses `_write_link_event` directly — bypassing `add_dependency`. This means 0b96's test does not validate the actual code path that 555e takes for cycle/blocking semantics, leaving the critical behavioral guarantee untested on the real execution path.
findings.location: child 0b96-cbcb-0ea2-479d, Tests: 'a `caused_by` two-cycle is allowed (asserts the `_graph.py` semantics)'; child 555e-3efa-4ad6-4169, Approach: 'writes via `graph._links._write_link_event(bug, culprit, "caused_by", tracker_dir)` directly'
scenarios: 0b96's test passes (add_dependency respects non-blocking tuple), but _write_link_event has its own validation that is never tested for caused_by semantics
A future refactor adds a guard to _write_link_event; caused_by edges start failing silently; no test catches it because 0b96 tests a different path

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the exact `--class` enum members (e.g., `regression | plan_defect | flaky | external | unknown`) in the plan so the executor can implement validation, error messages, and golden fixtures without invention.
evidence: "bug classification via a persisted close `--class` enum replacing today's validated-then-discarded free-text reason"
"a REQUIRED bounded `--class` enum"
"its error message must enumerate the allowed values"
ABSENCE: no list of enum members appears anywhere in the plan text
findings.finding: The `--class` enum values are never enumerated anywhere in the plan. The plan requires a 'REQUIRED bounded `--class` enum', states the error message 'must enumerate the allowed values', and specifies that `close_class` is persisted on the STATUS event — but the actual legal values (e.g., regression, plan_defect, flaky, …) are never listed. An executor implementing `transition.py` and the close guard cannot write the enum, the validation, the error message, or the golden-test fixtures without inventing values, which is a blocking ambiguity.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'bug classification via a persisted close `--class` enum'; Approach — 'Back-out / expand-contract for the bug-close CLI change (ed13)'; AC line 4
scenarios: Executor must invent enum members (e.g., decide whether 'flaky' is a valid class, whether 'external' is distinct from 'infra'), producing an implementation that diverges from the author's intent.
Golden-test fixtures for `rebar transition --help` and `rebar show <bug> --output json` cannot be written correctly without knowing the exact values.
The AC assertion `jq -e '.close_class'` passes on any string, so a wrong enum is not caught by the AC as written.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table or inline list in the Instrumentation section, e.g.: 'Allowed `--class` values: `regression` (a prior change introduced the bug), `plan_defect` (spec/design error), `flaky` (non-deterministic), `external` (third-party/infra), `unknown` (cannot determine). `unknown` is the catch-all required for migration.' This also lets the backfill heuristic specify which structural signals map to which class.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the precise skip rule for multi-file `file_impact` (e.g., 'if all blamed files converge on the same single commit, proceed; if any two files blame different commits, skip') so the executor has a deterministic implementation target.
evidence: "spans multiple files ambiguously, or blame cannot resolve a single commit, auto-derivation is skipped"
ABSENCE: no definition of 'ambiguously' — whether it means >1 file, or >1 distinct blamed commit across files
findings.finding: The multi-file ambiguity skip rule for blame derivation is incompletely specified. The plan says auto-derivation is skipped when a bug 'spans multiple files ambiguously', but 'ambiguously' is undefined. It is unclear whether >1 file in `file_impact` always triggers a skip, or only when the blamed commits differ across files (i.e., a single common ancestor exists → proceed; different commits → skip). An executor cannot deterministically implement the condition.
findings.location: Approach — 'Blame-derivation is best-effort and non-authoritative.' paragraph
scenarios: Executor implements 'any bug with >1 file_impact entry → skip', producing zero auto-derived edges for multi-file bugs even when all files blame the same commit.
Executor implements 'skip only when blamed commits differ', producing edges that may surprise users when file_impact has 3 files all blaming the same commit.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'spans multiple files ambiguously' with: 'if `file_impact` contains >1 file and `git blame <fix>~1` over those files does not converge to a single unique commit, skip auto-derivation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific tool and metric type for complexity (e.g., radon cyclomatic average per module) and clone detection (e.g., jscpd token-duplicate %) so the metric registry keys can be deterministically specified and the integration test can assert them by name.
evidence: "complexity/clone snapshots at sampled commits" in backfill track
"code-health metrics" named as one of the three lenses in Success Criteria
ABSENCE: no tool (radon, lizard, jscpd, etc.) or metric unit specified anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The complexity and clone metrics used for backfill snapshots are unspecified — no tool name, metric type (cyclomatic vs cognitive vs Halstead for complexity; token-based vs AST-based for clone), or threshold is stated. While these are in the 'optional adapter' backfill track, the going-forward snapshot writer (3275, core track) also implies complexity/clone are registered metrics, meaning the executor must choose a tool and unit without guidance.
findings.location: Context — 'code-health movement (coverage/complexity/clone) is computed ephemerally in CI'; Decomposition — 'complexity/clone snapshots at sampled commits'
scenarios: Executor picks radon cyclomatic complexity; a later executor picks cognitive complexity; the time-series is discontinuous and the metric registry key is ambiguous.
The `rebar metrics --output json` integration test cannot assert a specific key name (e.g., `code_health.complexity.cyclomatic_avg`) without the tool being fixed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend an existing one) that specifically verifies gate-sidecar `ERROR` outcome writing and that env/integration-diagnosis intervals are derivable from the event store — e.g., assert that a gate run ending with an error status produces a sidecar with `outcome=ERROR` and that the metrics reader can compute a non-zero diagnosis interval from a synthetic fixture.
evidence: Context: 'env/integration-diagnosis intervals (an infra failure starts a diagnosis, its next clean gate ends it)'
Success Criteria: 'Tier-C blindspots...are captured passively: env/integration-diagnosis via an ERROR outcome on the existing gate sidecars'
Decomposition Command track: 'gate-sidecar reader + ERROR path'
AC list: no AC item references the ERROR outcome, the gate-sidecar ERROR path, or diagnosis-interval derivability — only the general 'all registered metric keys present' integration test
findings.finding: Tier-C blindspot (a) — env/integration-diagnosis interval capture via gate-sidecar `ERROR` outcome — is a described deliverable with no dedicated acceptance criterion verifying it was implemented or that the signal can be derived.
findings.location: Success Criteria (Tier-C blindspots), Decomposition (Command track: 'gate-sidecar reader + ERROR path'), Context ('Tier-C blindspots' definition (a))
scenarios: The `ERROR` outcome write path is never implemented or tested; the metrics command registers the metric but always emits `unavailable`; the blindspot is declared closed but never actually captured.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC: 'The gate `ERROR` outcome path is verified by a unit test that writes a gate sidecar with `outcome=ERROR` and asserts the metrics reader computes a non-zero env/diagnosis interval for the corresponding window.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC 3/5) that verifies `--caused-by <id>` writes a `caused_by` edge with the specified target and that it takes precedence over any auto-derived edge, e.g., `rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --class regression --caused-by <id>` followed by `rebar show <bug> --output json | jq -e '.links[] | select(.relation=="caused_by") | .target == "<id>"'`.
evidence: Approach: 'the explicit `--caused-by <id>` override always wins'
Decomposition Instrumentation track: '--caused-by param + bug-template advisory' listed as a deliverable
AC list: no AC item tests `--caused-by` invocation, its precedence over auto-derivation, or that the written edge carries the correct source/confidence metadata
findings.finding: The `--caused-by <id>` explicit override parameter is described as a deliverable in the Approach and Decomposition (Instrumentation track) but has no acceptance criterion covering its behavior, error handling, or persistence.
findings.location: Approach ('the explicit `--caused-by <id>` override always wins'), Decomposition (Instrumentation: 'blame-based caused_by auto-derivation + `--caused-by` param + bug-template advisory')
scenarios: The `--caused-by` override is implemented incorrectly (e.g., still writes `confidence=classified` instead of something more authoritative, or loses the override on re-derivation); no AC catches the regression.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or sub-AC on ticket 3275) that asserts the snapshot file is written to `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` (confirmed git-tracked, absent from `.gitignore`) — e.g., a test that writes a snapshot and verifies `git ls-files .rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` returns the file.
evidence: 'The going-forward snapshot series (3275) is committed to `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` — a git-tracked path in the rebar store — NOT under `reports/`, which is gitignored (.gitignore:74) and would silently drop every snapshot.'
ABSENCE: No AC asserts that the snapshot file is written to `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` (git-tracked) rather than `reports/`.
findings.finding: The going-forward snapshot store path (`.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`) is a non-deferred goal with a documented failure mode (silent data loss if it lands under `reports/` which is gitignored), but no Acceptance Criterion verifies this property — there is no observable proof that snapshots are written to the correct git-tracked path.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Snapshot store path' paragraph; Decomposition — 'Command' track (child 3275); Acceptance Criteria (no AC covers snapshot path)
scenarios: If the snapshot writer is implemented against `reports/` by mistake, every snapshot is silently dropped and the going-forward series never accrues — this is the exact failure mode the plan warns about, but no test catches it.
A future refactor moves the path without realizing it must stay git-tracked; no AC fails to catch the regression.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '`rebar metrics` snapshot writer test: after `rebar metrics` runs, `git ls-files .rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` returns the file (confirming it is git-tracked and NOT under a gitignored path).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in `blame.py`'s design (ticket 555e) that `git blame <fix>~1 -- <path>` is guarded against files that do not exist at `<fix>~1` (newly-added files): detect via `git cat-file -e <fix>~1:<path>` before blaming, and return `None` (skip, no link) on any nonzero exit — adding this case to the documented skip conditions alongside the existing 'no file_impact / multi-commit / no trailer' list.
evidence: Plan states skip conditions: 'no file_impact, spans multiple files ambiguously, or blame cannot resolve a single commit'

Plan does NOT list: file newly added by the fixing commit (pre-fix parent has no such file → `git blame <fix>~1 -- <file>` exits nonzero or returns empty), binary files, files deleted/renamed between culprit and fix
src/rebar/metrics/blame.py does not exist yet — the plan is specifying behavior for a module to be written
ABSENCE: no integration test fixture covers the newly-added-file case specifically
findings.finding: The blame-based `caused_by` derivation asserts that `git blame <fix>~1 -- <path>` will reliably produce a resolvable culprit commit, but does not specify behavior for newly-added files (the pre-fix parent has no such file, so blame fails), binary files, or files deleted between the culprit commit and the pre-fix state — edge cases that arise naturally in any real codebase and that the `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` module (which does not yet exist) must handle.
findings.location: Approach section: 'Blame-derivation is best-effort and non-authoritative'; ticket 555e description: `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py`
scenarios: A bug fixes a newly-added function in a newly-added file: the pre-fix parent has no such file, git blame returns an error or empty output, and an unguarded blame call would raise rather than skipping
A bug fixes a line in a binary file: git blame on a binary file produces unreliable output
The culprit commit deleted a file that the fix re-adds: the pre-fix parent has no such path
findings.suggested_fix: In the `blame.py` spec, add: before invoking `git blame <fix>~1 -- <path>`, confirm the path exists at `<fix>~1` via `git cat-file -e <fix>~1:<path>` (exit 0 = exists, nonzero = newly added → skip). Document this as an additional skip condition in the plan's Approach section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Create (or surface the missing) child ticket for the gate-sidecar reader + ERROR-outcome emission path, specifying: which field on the REVIEW_RESULT/COMPLETION_VERDICT sidecar carries the ERROR outcome, which gate error-handler code writes it, and what the reader in `src/rebar/metrics/` derives from it — then link it as a dep of 9a5a.
evidence: 9a5a deps: {"target_id": "3c07-27ac-4b61-47a3", "relation": "depends_on"}
rebar show 3c07 → 'Ticket 3c07 not found'
Epic prose: 'env/integration-diagnosis via an ERROR outcome on the existing gate sidecars' — no child ticket defines which field/value is added to the sidecar schema
Search for REVIEW_RESULT and COMPLETION_VERDICT in source returns no matches, so the sidecar format cannot be verified
The Tier-C blindspot (a) is described as a deliverable but its implementation ticket is missing from the ticket store
findings.finding: The gate-sidecar reader (referenced as ticket '3c07' in 9a5a's `depends_on` links) does not exist as a retrievable ticket — `rebar show 3c07` returns 'Ticket not found'. The mechanism by which existing gate sidecars (REVIEW_RESULT, COMPLETION_VERDICT) acquire an `ERROR` outcome to capture env/integration-diagnosis intervals is described only in the epic prose ('via an ERROR outcome on the existing gate sidecars') with no child ticket specifying the sidecar schema change, the ERROR-path emission code, or the reader implementation. The REVIEW_RESULT and COMPLETION_VERDICT sidecar schemas are absent from the codebase search results, making it impossible to verify the ERROR outcome field exists or is planned.
findings.location: Epic Decomposition ('gate-sidecar reader + ERROR path'); 9a5a deps list (target_id: 3c07-27ac-4b61-47a3); Tier-C blindspot (a)
scenarios: The implementing agent cannot find ticket 3c07 and has no specification for what ERROR outcome means on a gate sidecar, what field it occupies, or how the reader detects it.
The sidecar schema may already have an outcome/verdict field — but without the ticket, the ERROR value and its gate-error-handler emission path are unspecified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the 555e ticket (or blame.py spec), define the exact disambiguation rule for 'single dominant culprit' — e.g., '100% of blame hunks in file_impact point to the same commit SHA' (strict) or '>= N% by line count' — so the resolver has a deterministic, testable criterion.
evidence: 555e Approach: 'Resolves the dominant culprit commit and maps it to a ticket via its rebar-ticket: trailer. Returns None (no link) on ambiguity: no file_impact, multi-commit spread, rename/--follow uncertainty, or no resolvable trailer.'
Epic Approach: 'When a bug has no file_impact, spans multiple files ambiguously, or blame cannot resolve a single commit, auto-derivation is skipped'
Neither the epic nor 555e defines what 'single' means quantitatively — is it 100% of lines, >50%, or majority by file count?
findings.finding: The blame 'single dominant culprit' disambiguation rule is referenced but its threshold is never quantified. The plan says auto-derivation skips on 'multi-commit spread' but does not define what fraction of blame hunks (or minimum percentage) must point to one commit for it to be considered 'single dominant'. Without a numeric rule, the resolver in `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` has no deterministic decision criterion for the boundary case (e.g., 60% from one commit, 40% from another).
findings.location: Epic Approach ('Blame-derivation is best-effort'); 555e ticket / Approach ('Resolves the dominant culprit commit ... Returns None on ambiguity: ... multi-commit spread')
scenarios: A file has 70 lines blamed to commit A and 30 to commit B; one implementer treats this as 'single dominant' (A wins), another as 'ambiguous' (multi-commit spread) — producing different blame.py behaviors.
A regression spans 3 files with 2 files blaming A and 1 blaming B; file-majority vs line-majority produce different results.
findings.suggested_fix: The safest rule consistent with the plan's conservative posture is: all non-whitespace blame hunks across all file_impact paths must resolve to the same commit SHA; any divergence → skip (return None). This is strict, falsifiable, and matches 'single culprit'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit dependency from 2f8e on 555e (or document the landing order: 555e before 2f8e) and assert `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` exists in 2f8e's test setup.
evidence: 2f8e Approach: 'Reuse the same conservative single-culprit resolver as the live auto-derivation... reuse `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py`'
555e Files: '`src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` — new: single-culprit resolver'
Neither child's dependencies section names the other
Parent decomposition does not encode this ordering in the dep graph ('shared-file ordering the dep graph does not encode')
findings.finding: Child 2f8e depends on `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` created by child 555e, but declares no dependency on 555e. If 2f8e lands before 555e (or is reviewed/tested in isolation), the backfill script fails immediately with a missing module import.
findings.location: child 2f8e-0f7e-6fea-4825, Approach: 'Reuse `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py`'
scenarios: 2f8e is landed or tested before 555e; `scripts/backfill_caused_by.py` imports `rebar.metrics.blame` which does not exist; CI fails on import
A reviewer testing 2f8e in isolation finds the module missing and cannot distinguish a real bug from a missing dep
findings.suggested_fix: Add '555e must be merged before 2f8e' to 2f8e's approach section, and have `tests/unit/scripts/test_backfill_caused_by.py` import `rebar.metrics.blame` at the top level so CI immediately surfaces the missing dep rather than failing mid-test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign ownership of the registration seam to c085 (e.g., a `register_metric(spec)` function or per-module `_register()` call pattern) so 18e6, 3c07, and 7931 each call it without directly editing the REGISTRY dict.
evidence: Parent cross-ticket coordination: 'Layer-1 reader children (18e6, 3c07, 7931) each register into `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py`; land them sequentially up the stack (or via an append-only registration seam) so registry edits do not collide'
18e6 Files: 'registered in `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py`'
3c07 Files: 'registered in `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py`'
7931 Files: 'registered in `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py`'
c085 (registry owner) does not define a plugin/registration seam — only defines the REGISTRY dict and MetricSpec
findings.finding: No child owns the append-only registration seam for `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py`. Children 18e6, 3c07, and 7931 each register metrics into this shared file, and the parent warns of edit collisions, but none of the three children defines or owns the seam mechanism (plugin pattern, separate files, or registration function), leaving the coordination gap unresolved.
findings.location: children 18e6-4bfb-1dcf-4a37, 3c07-27ac-4b61-47a3, 7931-e644-30de-4023 — all edit `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py`
scenarios: All three reader children are developed in parallel; each edits registry.py to append to REGISTRY; merge conflicts arise on the same dict literal
Sequential landing still requires careful rebase; without a seam, the last-merged child must manually incorporate all prior registrations

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit 0b96 dependency declaration to 555e, and have 555e's integration test assert that 'caused_by' is present in CANONICAL_RELATIONS as a precondition check.
evidence: 555e Approach: 'The auto-link therefore writes via `graph._links._write_link_event(bug, culprit, "caused_by", tracker_dir)` directly'
0b96 Files: '`src/rebar/graph/_links.py` — add `caused_by` to `CANONICAL_RELATIONS`'
Parent ordering: '0b96 lands first (Layer 0)' — but 555e's AC does not assert 0b96 is a precondition
555e AC#1 tests the round-trip but not specifically that _write_link_event accepts the relation string without 0b96's CANONICAL_RELATIONS change
findings.finding: Child 555e uses `graph._links._write_link_event` directly to bypass the closed-source guard, but this private writer may validate the relation string against `CANONICAL_RELATIONS` (defined in 0b96's `_links.py` change). If 0b96 is not yet merged when 555e is tested, `caused_by` is not in `CANONICAL_RELATIONS` and the direct write fails — yet neither child's AC explicitly tests or enforces this integration path.
findings.location: child 555e-3efa-4ad6-4169, Approach: 'writes via `graph._links._write_link_event(bug, culprit, "caused_by", tracker_dir)` directly'; child 0b96-cbcb-0ea2-479d, Files: 'add `caused_by` to `CANONICAL_RELATIONS`'
scenarios: 555e is tested in isolation or before 0b96; `_write_link_event` validates relation against CANONICAL_RELATIONS; 'caused_by' is unknown; auto-link fails silently or raises
Integration test for 555e passes locally (0b96 already merged) but the ordering dependency is invisible to reviewers

findings.finding: The git blame integration for caused_by auto-derivation (blame.py, ticket 555e) introduces a new external git CLI surface with unverified behavioral specifics: the plan does not specify which git blame flags are used (e.g., --porcelain, -l, -C for copy detection), how 'dominant culprit commit' is operationalized from the per-line blame output (the plan says 'single dominant culprit' but gives no algorithm), or what happens when a file_impact path was deleted/renamed between fix~1 and the blame invocation (requires --follow or explicit error handling). Git blame has no prior use in this codebase (search confirmed zero hits), so there is no precedent to inherit from.
findings.location: Approach section ('Blame-derivation is best-effort and non-authoritative'), ticket 555e (blame.py description), ticket 2f8e (backfill_caused_by.py)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ticket 555e's Approach: 'blame.py uses `git blame --porcelain <fix>~1 -- <path>` per file_impact entry; dominant culprit = the commit SHA appearing in >50% of blamed lines (else skip); a non-zero git blame exit or empty output skips the file silently.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
